item.title:
FOOD PRICES CLIMB AS WAR DRIVES COSTS HIGHER
Global food prices rose in March, pushed up by higher energy and freight costs linked to conflict in the Middle East.
The UN’s food price index averaged 128.5 points, up 2.4% from February—its second straight monthly increase after five months of decline.
The rise reflects growing pressure across supply chains, as the conflict drives up fuel and fertilizer costs and disrupts key trade routes like the Strait of Hormuz.
Vegetable oil and sugar saw the biggest price jumps, with meat, dairy, and cereals also increasing—signaling that food inflation may persist.
item.description:
FOOD PRICES CLIMB AS WAR DRIVES COSTS HIGHER

Global food prices rose in March, pushed up by higher energy and freight costs linked to conflict in the Middle East.

The UN’s food price index averaged 128.5 points, up 2.4% from February—its second straight monthly increase after five months of decline.

The rise reflects growing pressure across supply chains, as the conflict drives up fuel and fertilizer costs and disrupts key trade routes like the Strait of Hormuz.

Vegetable oil and sugar saw the biggest price jumps, with meat, dairy, and cereals also increasing—signaling that food inflation may persist.

item.title:
TRUMP THREATENS FURTHER DESTRUCTION, URGES DEAL
Trump claimed a major Iranian bridge had been destroyed and warned that more strikes are coming.
He urged Iran to make a deal quickly, signaling that further escalation could leave the country severely damaged.
item.description:
TRUMP THREATENS FURTHER DESTRUCTION, URGES DEAL

Trump claimed a major Iranian bridge had been destroyed and warned that more strikes are coming.

He urged Iran to make a deal quickly, signaling that further escalation could leave the country severely damaged.

item.title:
MORTGAGE RATES HIT HIGHEST LEVEL SINCE SEPTEMBER
Mortgage rates rose for a fifth straight week, with the 30-year fixed reaching 6.46%, the highest since September.
Rising bond yields—driven by inflation fears linked to the Iran war—are pushing borrowing costs higher.
The increase is cooling the housing market, with purchase applications down 3% and buyer activity lagging behind last year amid growing economic uncertainty.
item.description:
MORTGAGE RATES HIT HIGHEST LEVEL SINCE SEPTEMBER

Mortgage rates rose for a fifth straight week, with the 30-year fixed reaching 6.46%, the highest since September.

Rising bond yields—driven by inflation fears linked to the Iran war—are pushing borrowing costs higher.

The increase is cooling the housing market, with purchase applications down 3% and buyer activity lagging behind last year amid growing economic uncertainty.

item.title: *US MORTGAGE RATES RISE FOR FIFTH WEEK, SENDING 30-YEAR TO 6.46%
item.description: *US MORTGAGE RATES RISE FOR FIFTH WEEK, SENDING 30-YEAR TO 6.46%

item.title:
TRUMP SIGNALS PROLONGED IRAN CONFLICT
Trump’s latest speech points to several more weeks of fighting, with analysts warning the risk of US ground involvement remains high.
They cite the ongoing military buildup and broad war objectives as key factors, and note the timing before the holiday leaves room for potential escalation in the coming days.
item.description:
TRUMP SIGNALS PROLONGED IRAN CONFLICT

Trump’s latest speech points to several more weeks of fighting, with analysts warning the risk of US ground involvement remains high.

They cite the ongoing military buildup and broad war objectives as key factors, and note the timing before the holiday leaves room for potential escalation in the coming days.

item.title:
MARKETS TURN BEARISH ON HORMUZ REOPENING
Prediction markets show declining confidence that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz will normalize soon. Odds of reopening before June 1 have fallen to around 30%, down sharply from recent highs.
The trend reflects growing expectations of prolonged disruption, reinforcing pressure on global energy markets. https://kalshi.com/markets/kxhormuznorm/when-will-traffic-at-the-strait-of-hormuz-return-to-normal/kxhormuznorm-26mar17?utm_source=walterbloomberg
item.description:
MARKETS TURN BEARISH ON HORMUZ REOPENING

Prediction markets show declining confidence that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz will normalize soon. Odds of reopening before June 1 have fallen to around 30%, down sharply from recent highs.

The trend reflects growing expectations of prolonged disruption, reinforcing pressure on global energy markets.
kalshi.com/markets/kxhormuzn…

item.title:
$TSLA - TESLA MISSES Q1 DELIVERY EXPECTATIONS
Tesla reported Q1 deliveries of 358,023 vehicles, below estimates of 368,903, despite producing 408,386 units.
Model 3/Y dominated output with 394,611 produced and 341,893 delivered, while other models saw 13,775 produced and 16,130 delivered.
The company also deployed 8.8 GWh of energy storage in the quarter.
item.description:
$TSLA - TESLA MISSES Q1 DELIVERY EXPECTATIONS

Tesla reported Q1 deliveries of 358,023 vehicles, below estimates of 368,903, despite producing 408,386 units.

Model 3/Y dominated output with 394,611 produced and 341,893 delivered, while other models saw 13,775 produced and 16,130 delivered.

The company also deployed 8.8 GWh of energy storage in the quarter.

item.title:
$TSLA
TESLA 1Q MODEL 3/Y DELIVERIES 341,893, EST. 353,928
TESLA 1Q MODEL 3/Y PRODUCTION 394,611, EST. 377,147
TESLA 1Q OTHER MODELS PRODUCTION 13,775, +18% Q/Q
TESLA 1Q OTHER MODELS DELIVERIES 16,130, EST. 12,393
item.description:
$TSLA

TESLA 1Q MODEL 3/Y DELIVERIES 341,893, EST. 353,928

TESLA 1Q MODEL 3/Y PRODUCTION 394,611, EST. 377,147

TESLA 1Q OTHER MODELS PRODUCTION 13,775, +18% Q/Q

TESLA 1Q OTHER MODELS DELIVERIES 16,130, EST. 12,393

item.title:
OIL SURGES PAST $110 AS WAR RISKS INTENSIFY
Oil jumped above $110 and diesel topped $200 after Trump threatened escalation in Iran, raising fears of prolonged disruption through the Strait of Hormuz.
Despite hints the war could end soon, no clear timeline or plan has emerged, keeping markets on edge. With the strait effectively closed, supply remains severely constrained and prices have surged—Brent is up about 50% since the conflict began.
Refined fuels are under even more pressure, with diesel spikes signaling rising global inflation risks. Tight supply is evident, with near-term oil contracts trading at record premiums.
Until there’s clarity on de-escalation or Hormuz reopens, analysts expect continued volatility and elevated energy prices.
item.description:
OIL SURGES PAST $110 AS WAR RISKS INTENSIFY

Oil jumped above $110 and diesel topped $200 after Trump threatened escalation in Iran, raising fears of prolonged disruption through the Strait of Hormuz.

Despite hints the war could end soon, no clear timeline or plan has emerged, keeping markets on edge. With the strait effectively closed, supply remains severely constrained and prices have surged—Brent is up about 50% since the conflict began.

Refined fuels are under even more pressure, with diesel spikes signaling rising global inflation risks. Tight supply is evident, with near-term oil contracts trading at record premiums.

Until there’s clarity on de-escalation or Hormuz reopens, analysts expect continued volatility and elevated energy prices.

item.description: Macron says Trump's comments about his relationship are "neither elegant nor up to standard"

item.title:
BLACKROCK BETS ON RISING EUROPEAN YIELDS
BlackRock is increasing short positions on German bonds, expecting a sharp rise in inflation to push yields above recent 15-year highs.
The firm sees higher government spending on energy support and defense driving more debt issuance, increasing bond supply and borrowing costs.
With Europe heavily exposed to energy shocks, inflation risks are seen as greater than in the US or UK. BlackRock expects German 10-year yields to climb further from around 3%.
The shift in outlook—fewer rate cuts and potential hikes—has already paid off, with the fund outperforming peers as global yields rise.
item.description:
BLACKROCK BETS ON RISING EUROPEAN YIELDS

BlackRock is increasing short positions on German bonds, expecting a sharp rise in inflation to push yields above recent 15-year highs.

The firm sees higher government spending on energy support and defense driving more debt issuance, increasing bond supply and borrowing costs.

With Europe heavily exposed to energy shocks, inflation risks are seen as greater than in the US or UK. BlackRock expects German 10-year yields to climb further from around 3%.

The shift in outlook—fewer rate cuts and potential hikes—has already paid off, with the fund outperforming peers as global yields rise.

item.description: *JAPAN DECIDES TO JOIN UK-LED ONLINE TALKS ON HORMUZ: REUTERS

item.title:
GLOBAL DIESEL SCRAMBLE SENDS SHIPS ON EXTREME ROUTES
Surging fuel prices from the Iran war are forcing traders into unusually long and costly shipping routes. Tankers are now traveling over 12,000 miles—like a UK-loaded vessel heading to Australia—as higher Asian prices pull fuel away from Europe.
Despite Europe facing diesel shortages, Asia’s supply crunch is even worse, driving a global tug-of-war for fuel. The disruption is fueled by the Strait of Hormuz closure and reduced refining, creating what the IEA calls a record supply shock.
Unprecedented flows are emerging, with multiple tankers rerouting from the US and Europe to Australia and Asia. The longer the Hormuz disruption lasts, the tighter global supply becomes—raising the risk of shortages, especially in Europe.
item.description:
GLOBAL DIESEL SCRAMBLE SENDS SHIPS ON EXTREME ROUTES

Surging fuel prices from the Iran war are forcing traders into unusually long and costly shipping routes. Tankers are now traveling over 12,000 miles—like a UK-loaded vessel heading to Australia—as higher Asian prices pull fuel away from Europe.

Despite Europe facing diesel shortages, Asia’s supply crunch is even worse, driving a global tug-of-war for fuel. The disruption is fueled by the Strait of Hormuz closure and reduced refining, creating what the IEA calls a record supply shock.

Unprecedented flows are emerging, with multiple tankers rerouting from the US and Europe to Australia and Asia. The longer the Hormuz disruption lasts, the tighter global supply becomes—raising the risk of shortages, especially in Europe.

item.title:
IRAN WARNS OF TOTAL RESPONSE AS US BUILDS FORCES
Iran has escalated its rhetoric after Trump’s overnight threats. Army chief Amir Hatami warned that any ground invasion would face total destruction.
Meanwhile, the US has deployed thousands of troops to the region, raising speculation of a possible ground operation.
item.description:
IRAN WARNS OF TOTAL RESPONSE AS US BUILDS FORCES

Iran has escalated its rhetoric after Trump’s overnight threats. Army chief Amir Hatami warned that any ground invasion would face total destruction.

Meanwhile, the US has deployed thousands of troops to the region, raising speculation of a possible ground operation.

item.title: IRANIAN ARMY CHIEF: NO ENEMY MUST ESCAPE IF THEY ATTEMPT TO CARRY OUT A GROUND OPERATION
item.description: IRANIAN ARMY CHIEF: NO ENEMY MUST ESCAPE IF THEY ATTEMPT TO CARRY OUT A GROUND OPERATION

item.title:
HORMUZ UNCERTAINTY KEEPS OIL PRICES HIGH
Trump’s latest remarks have not changed forecasts, with Brent still expected to average around $113 in Q2, according to Oxford Economics.
The main risk remains the Strait of Hormuz. With no clear plan to reopen it, supply disruptions are likely to continue, keeping upward pressure on oil prices.
Analysts warn that the longer the strait stays effectively closed, the less effective reserves become—raising costs for the global economy.
item.description:
HORMUZ UNCERTAINTY KEEPS OIL PRICES HIGH

Trump’s latest remarks have not changed forecasts, with Brent still expected to average around $113 in Q2, according to Oxford Economics.

The main risk remains the Strait of Hormuz. With no clear plan to reopen it, supply disruptions are likely to continue, keeping upward pressure on oil prices.

Analysts warn that the longer the strait stays effectively closed, the less effective reserves become—raising costs for the global economy.

item.title: *EUROPEAN DIESEL FUTURES HIT $200 A BARREL AS WAR CURBS SUPPLY
item.description: *EUROPEAN DIESEL FUTURES HIT $200 A BARREL AS WAR CURBS SUPPLY

item.title:
METALS SLIDE AS WAR UNCERTAINTY SHAKES MARKETS
Gold and silver fell sharply after Trump’s remarks failed to provide a clear end to the war. Gold dropped about 3% to around $4,670/oz, while silver fell over 5%.
The selloff reflects rising inflation fears from higher oil prices, which could push central banks to keep rates higher for longer—bad news for non-yielding assets like gold. Prices are also down roughly 11% since the war began.
Analysts say some investors are locking in profits ahead of the long Easter weekend, adding to the decline.
item.description:
METALS SLIDE AS WAR UNCERTAINTY SHAKES MARKETS

Gold and silver fell sharply after Trump’s remarks failed to provide a clear end to the war. Gold dropped about 3% to around $4,670/oz, while silver fell over 5%.

The selloff reflects rising inflation fears from higher oil prices, which could push central banks to keep rates higher for longer—bad news for non-yielding assets like gold. Prices are also down roughly 11% since the war began.

Analysts say some investors are locking in profits ahead of the long Easter weekend, adding to the decline.

item.title:
ALTMAN'S SISTER FILES AMENDED LAWSUIT ALLEGING SEXUAL ABUSE
Annie Altman has filed an amended lawsuit accusing her brother, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, of long-term sexual abuse. A judge allowed the case to proceed under Missouri’s child abuse law, even though other claims were ruled too old.
She alleges abuse from 1997 to 2006, beginning when she was three years old. Sam Altman denies the allegations and has filed a defamation countersuit based on her public statements.
The Altman family disputes her claims, citing mental health concerns and describing the lawsuit as extortion.
item.description:
ALTMAN'S SISTER FILES AMENDED LAWSUIT ALLEGING SEXUAL ABUSE

Annie Altman has filed an amended lawsuit accusing her brother, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, of long-term sexual abuse. A judge allowed the case to proceed under Missouri’s child abuse law, even though other claims were ruled too old.

She alleges abuse from 1997 to 2006, beginning when she was three years old. Sam Altman denies the allegations and has filed a defamation countersuit based on her public statements.

The Altman family disputes her claims, citing mental health concerns and describing the lawsuit as extortion.
